---
title: "Programming the GPU"
---

Why open a numerics library's documentation with a chapter on hardware? Because
`nitrix` is fast for reasons that are invisible in the Python source, and that
become obvious the moment you can see the machine underneath. Two ideas from this
chapter — the **memory hierarchy** and the **roofline** — are the whole reason the
next chapter's compiler optimisations exist, and the reason the geometry and
registration units each close with a performance chapter about *when a fused kernel
pays off*. You cannot reason about "fast" on a GPU without them.

The plan is to build the machine up in the order its design decisions cascade. A
GPU is built for **throughput, not latency**; that single choice forces the
**SIMT** execution model; that model pays off only if the machine stays fed, which
forces a steep **memory hierarchy**; and the gap between how fast the arithmetic
units run and how fast memory can feed them is captured by the **roofline**, the
mental model you reach for every time you ask "why is this slow?". We close with
the **tensor cores**, the **generations** of NVIDIA hardware, and what of all this
`nitrix` actually targets.

## Latency versus throughput: two ways to be fast {#sec-throughput}

There are two different things "a fast processor" can mean, and a CPU and a GPU
pick opposite answers.

A **CPU** is built to finish *one* task as quickly as possible — to minimise
**latency**. It spends its transistor budget on a handful of powerful cores, each
with deep caches, branch prediction, and out-of-order, speculative machinery, all
devoted to racing a single thread of instructions to the finish line. If your
problem is "run this mostly-sequential program with unpredictable branches," a CPU
is the right machine.

A **GPU** is built to finish *an enormous number* of similar tasks per second — to
maximise **throughput**. It spends the same budget on thousands of small, simple
arithmetic units and almost nothing on per-thread cleverness. Any individual
operation is *slower* to complete than on a CPU; the win is having thousands of
them in flight at once. The GPU's strategy for the long wait on slow memory is not
to avoid it (caches) or predict around it (speculation) but to **hide** it: when
one group of threads stalls waiting for data, the scheduler instantly swaps in
another group that is ready to compute. Keep enough work resident and the
arithmetic units never idle.

@fig-cpu-gpu draws the contrast. The practical consequence for everything below: a
GPU is fast only when you give it **massive, regular, data-parallel work** — the
same operation over millions of array elements — while keeping the memory system
saturated so latency stays hidden. `nitrix`'s mass-univariate workloads (the same
small model fit at every one of $10^5$–$10^6$ voxels; the same warp applied at
every voxel of a volume) are exactly this shape.

::: {#fig-cpu-gpu}
<svg viewBox="0 0 800 300" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img"
     aria-label="A CPU spends its area on a few large cores and big caches to minimise the latency of one thread; a GPU spends its area on many small cores to maximise the throughput of many threads."
     font-family="system-ui, sans-serif" font-size="13">
  <text x="180" y="26" text-anchor="middle" font-size="15" font-weight="bold">CPU — minimise latency</text>
  <rect x="30" y="40" width="300" height="222" rx="8" fill="#f4f6f8" stroke="#33373d"/>
  <rect x="46" y="56" width="120" height="78" rx="5" fill="#dfe7f0" stroke="#33373d"/>
  <rect x="194" y="56" width="120" height="78" rx="5" fill="#dfe7f0" stroke="#33373d"/>
  <rect x="46" y="146" width="120" height="40" rx="5" fill="#dfe7f0" stroke="#33373d"/>
  <rect x="194" y="146" width="120" height="40" rx="5" fill="#dfe7f0" stroke="#33373d"/>
  <text x="106" y="100" text-anchor="middle">core</text>
  <text x="254" y="100" text-anchor="middle">core</text>
  <rect x="46" y="198" width="268" height="48" rx="5" fill="#fbf0d6" stroke="#33373d"/>
  <text x="180" y="226" text-anchor="middle">large cache</text>
  <text x="180" y="284" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12" fill="#444b53" font-style="italic">a few powerful cores, deep caches</text>
  <text x="600" y="26" text-anchor="middle" font-size="15" font-weight="bold">GPU — maximise throughput</text>
  <rect x="450" y="40" width="320" height="222" rx="8" fill="#f4f6f8" stroke="#33373d"/>
  <g fill="#dce9dd" stroke="#33373d">
    <rect x="466" y="56" width="28" height="28" rx="3"/><rect x="500" y="56" width="28" height="28" rx="3"/><rect x="534" y="56" width="28" height="28" rx="3"/><rect x="568" y="56" width="28" height="28" rx="3"/><rect x="602" y="56" width="28" height="28" rx="3"/><rect x="636" y="56" width="28" height="28" rx="3"/><rect x="670" y="56" width="28" height="28" rx="3"/><rect x="704" y="56" width="28" height="28" rx="3"/>
    <rect x="466" y="92" width="28" height="28" rx="3"/><rect x="500" y="92" width="28" height="28" rx="3"/><rect x="534" y="92" width="28" height="28" rx="3"/><rect x="568" y="92" width="28" height="28" rx="3"/><rect x="602" y="92" width="28" height="28" rx="3"/><rect x="636" y="92" width="28" height="28" rx="3"/><rect x="670" y="92" width="28" height="28" rx="3"/><rect x="704" y="92" width="28" height="28" rx="3"/>
    <rect x="466" y="128" width="28" height="28" rx="3"/><rect x="500" y="128" width="28" height="28" rx="3"/><rect x="534" y="128" width="28" height="28" rx="3"/><rect x="568" y="128" width="28" height="28" rx="3"/><rect x="602" y="128" width="28" height="28" rx="3"/><rect x="636" y="128" width="28" height="28" rx="3"/><rect x="670" y="128" width="28" height="28" rx="3"/><rect x="704" y="128" width="28" height="28" rx="3"/>
    <rect x="466" y="164" width="28" height="28" rx="3"/><rect x="500" y="164" width="28" height="28" rx="3"/><rect x="534" y="164" width="28" height="28" rx="3"/><rect x="568" y="164" width="28" height="28" rx="3"/><rect x="602" y="164" width="28" height="28" rx="3"/><rect x="636" y="164" width="28" height="28" rx="3"/><rect x="670" y="164" width="28" height="28" rx="3"/><rect x="704" y="164" width="28" height="28" rx="3"/>
  </g>
  <rect x="466" y="200" width="266" height="46" rx="5" fill="#fbf0d6" stroke="#33373d"/>
  <text x="599" y="227" text-anchor="middle">small caches</text>
  <text x="600" y="284" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12" fill="#444b53" font-style="italic">thousands of small cores, latency hidden by oversubscription</text>
</svg>

Two ways to spend a transistor budget. The CPU (left) puts its area
into a few large cores and big caches to race one thread to the finish; the GPU
(right) fills its area with many small arithmetic units to keep thousands of
threads in flight, hiding memory latency by sheer oversubscription.
:::

## The SIMT execution model {#sec-simt}

Throughput-first hardware needs a programming model that exposes thousands of
identical, independent units of work. NVIDIA's is **SIMT** — *single instruction,
multiple threads* — and it has a strict hierarchy worth knowing by name, because it
shapes how every kernel in `nitrix` is written.

You write a **kernel**: the body of work for *one* thread, as if it were an
ordinary scalar program. At launch you ask for a **grid** of **thread blocks**, and
each block holds many **threads**. The hardware then schedules threads in groups of
**32 called a warp**, and this is the load-bearing detail: the 32 lanes of a warp
execute *the same instruction at the same time*, each on its own data. One
instruction, thirty-two results. @fig-simt shows the hierarchy.

Two consequences fall straight out of "32 lanes share one instruction," and both
recur in `nitrix`'s kernel design.

- **Branch divergence is expensive.** If a conditional sends some lanes of a warp
  down the `if` and the rest down the `else`, the warp cannot run both at once. It
  runs the `if` with the `else`-lanes *masked off* (idle), then the `else` with the
  `if`-lanes masked — the two paths are **serialised**. Data-dependent branching
  inside a warp throws away the parallelism it was meant to exploit, which is why
  GPU kernels prefer branch-free, uniform control flow. (It is also why JAX's
  compiled programs want *static* shapes and fixed iteration counts, the subject of
  the [next chapter](xla-jax.qmd#sec-control-flow).)
- **Occupancy hides latency.** A warp waiting on a memory load is not a stall if
  the scheduler has *other* warps ready to compute. The ratio of resident warps to
  the maximum the hardware can hold is called **occupancy**, and high occupancy is
  what lets the GPU paper over the hundreds-of-cycles wait for global memory. This
  is also why a kernel that uses too many registers or too much shared memory *per
  thread* can be slow even if its arithmetic is cheap: it limits how many warps fit,
  and the latency stops being hidden. (You will see this exact trade in the
  registration unit's Pallas demons kernel, whose tile size is capped precisely so a
  warp's working set fits in registers.)

::: {#fig-simt}
<svg viewBox="0 0 800 400" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img"
     aria-label="A launch is a grid of thread blocks; each block is scheduled as warps of 32 threads that execute one instruction in lockstep; a divergent branch serialises the two paths."
     font-family="system-ui, sans-serif" font-size="13">
  <defs>
    <marker id="hwah" viewBox="0 0 10 10" refX="9" refY="5" markerWidth="7" markerHeight="7" orient="auto-start-reverse">
      <path d="M0,0 L10,5 L0,10 z" fill="#55606b"/>
    </marker>
  </defs>
  <!-- grid -->
  <text x="100" y="26" text-anchor="middle" font-weight="bold">grid</text>
  <rect x="24" y="36" width="152" height="150" rx="6" fill="#f4f6f8" stroke="#33373d"/>
  <g fill="#dfe7f0" stroke="#33373d">
    <rect x="36" y="48" width="40" height="40" rx="3"/><rect x="84" y="48" width="40" height="40" rx="3"/><rect x="132" y="48" width="40" height="40" rx="3"/>
    <rect x="36" y="96" width="40" height="40" rx="3"/><rect x="84" y="96" width="40" height="40" rx="3"/><rect x="132" y="96" width="40" height="40" rx="3"/>
    <rect x="36" y="144" width="40" height="34" rx="3"/><rect x="84" y="144" width="40" height="34" rx="3"/><rect x="132" y="144" width="40" height="34" rx="3"/>
  </g>
  <text x="100" y="200" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#444b53">blocks</text>
  <line x1="176" y1="110" x2="232" y2="110" stroke="#55606b" stroke-width="1.4" marker-end="url(#hwah)"/>
  <!-- one block -> warps -->
  <text x="370" y="26" text-anchor="middle" font-weight="bold">one block</text>
  <rect x="240" y="36" width="260" height="150" rx="6" fill="#f4f6f8" stroke="#33373d"/>
  <rect x="252" y="52" width="236" height="52" rx="4" fill="#dce9dd" stroke="#33373d"/>
  <rect x="252" y="116" width="236" height="52" rx="4" fill="#dce9dd" stroke="#33373d"/>
  <text x="370" y="83" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12">warp (32 threads)</text>
  <text x="370" y="147" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12">warp (32 threads)</text>
  <line x1="500" y1="78" x2="556" y2="78" stroke="#55606b" stroke-width="1.4" marker-end="url(#hwah)"/>
  <!-- one warp = lanes in lockstep -->
  <text x="650" y="26" text-anchor="middle" font-weight="bold">one warp</text>
  <text x="650" y="44" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#444b53">1 instruction → 32 lanes, lockstep</text>
  <g fill="#e8ddec" stroke="#33373d">
    <rect x="560" y="52" width="16" height="26" rx="2"/><rect x="578" y="52" width="16" height="26" rx="2"/><rect x="596" y="52" width="16" height="26" rx="2"/><rect x="614" y="52" width="16" height="26" rx="2"/><rect x="632" y="52" width="16" height="26" rx="2"/><rect x="650" y="52" width="16" height="26" rx="2"/><rect x="668" y="52" width="16" height="26" rx="2"/><rect x="686" y="52" width="16" height="26" rx="2"/><rect x="704" y="52" width="16" height="26" rx="2"/><rect x="722" y="52" width="16" height="26" rx="2"/><rect x="740" y="52" width="16" height="26" rx="2"/>
    <rect x="560" y="82" width="16" height="26" rx="2"/><rect x="578" y="82" width="16" height="26" rx="2"/><rect x="596" y="82" width="16" height="26" rx="2"/><rect x="614" y="82" width="16" height="26" rx="2"/><rect x="632" y="82" width="16" height="26" rx="2"/><rect x="650" y="82" width="16" height="26" rx="2"/><rect x="668" y="82" width="16" height="26" rx="2"/><rect x="686" y="82" width="16" height="26" rx="2"/><rect x="704" y="82" width="16" height="26" rx="2"/><rect x="722" y="82" width="16" height="26" rx="2"/><rect x="740" y="82" width="16" height="26" rx="2"/>
  </g>
  <text x="650" y="128" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#444b53">… 32 lanes …</text>
  <!-- divergence panel -->
  <text x="400" y="232" text-anchor="middle" font-weight="bold">branch divergence within a warp</text>
  <rect x="120" y="248" width="560" height="120" rx="6" fill="#fdf6f6" stroke="#33373d"/>
  <text x="150" y="276" font-size="12">step 1: run the <tspan font-style="italic">if</tspan> path</text>
  <g>
    <rect x="150" y="286" width="20" height="22" fill="#dce9dd" stroke="#33373d"/><rect x="172" y="286" width="20" height="22" fill="#dce9dd" stroke="#33373d"/><rect x="194" y="286" width="20" height="22" fill="#cfcfcf" stroke="#33373d"/><rect x="216" y="286" width="20" height="22" fill="#cfcfcf" stroke="#33373d"/><rect x="238" y="286" width="20" height="22" fill="#dce9dd" stroke="#33373d"/><rect x="260" y="286" width="20" height="22" fill="#cfcfcf" stroke="#33373d"/>
    <text x="296" y="302" font-size="11" fill="#444b53">else-lanes masked (idle)</text>
  </g>
  <text x="150" y="338" font-size="12">step 2: run the <tspan font-style="italic">else</tspan> path</text>
  <g>
    <rect x="150" y="346" width="20" height="22" fill="#cfcfcf" stroke="#33373d"/><rect x="172" y="346" width="20" height="22" fill="#cfcfcf" stroke="#33373d"/><rect x="194" y="346" width="20" height="22" fill="#e8ddec" stroke="#33373d"/><rect x="216" y="346" width="20" height="22" fill="#e8ddec" stroke="#33373d"/><rect x="238" y="346" width="20" height="22" fill="#cfcfcf" stroke="#33373d"/><rect x="260" y="346" width="20" height="22" fill="#e8ddec" stroke="#33373d"/>
    <text x="296" y="362" font-size="11" fill="#444b53">if-lanes masked → the two paths are serialised</text>
  </g>
</svg>

The SIMT hierarchy. A launch is a *grid* of *blocks*;
each block is scheduled as *warps* of 32 threads that issue one instruction
in lockstep. When a branch splits a warp (bottom), the hardware runs the
*if* path with the other lanes masked, then the *else* path — the two
sides are serialised, so divergence costs parallelism.
:::

## The memory hierarchy: where the time actually goes {#sec-memory}

Here is the fact that governs GPU performance more than any other: **arithmetic is
cheap and moving data is expensive.** A fused-multiply-add costs a cycle or two; a
read from the GPU's main memory (HBM — high-bandwidth DRAM, the "VRAM" on a card's
spec sheet) costs *hundreds* of cycles. The whole machine is arranged as a
**hierarchy** that trades capacity for speed: tiny, blazing storage close to the
arithmetic units, and large, slow storage far away. @fig-memory is the standard
picture.

::: {#fig-memory}
<svg viewBox="0 0 800 380" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img"
     aria-label="The GPU memory hierarchy as a pyramid: registers at the narrow fast top, then shared memory and L1, L2 cache, HBM global memory, and host RAM at the wide slow bottom."
     font-family="system-ui, sans-serif" font-size="13">
  <!-- pyramid trapezoids, narrow (fast) top to wide (slow) bottom -->
  <polygon points="330,40 470,40 450,96 350,96" fill="#dfe7f0" stroke="#33373d"/>
  <polygon points="350,100 450,100 430,156 370,156" fill="#dfe7f0" stroke="#33373d"/>
  <polygon points="305,40 330,40 350,96 350,156 370,156 430,156 450,156 450,96 470,40 495,40 520,220 280,220" fill="none" stroke="none"/>
  <polygon points="296,160 504,160 524,216 276,216" fill="#dce9dd" stroke="#33373d"/>
  <polygon points="272,220 528,220 548,276 252,276" fill="#fbf0d6" stroke="#33373d"/>
  <polygon points="248,280 552,280 572,336 228,336" fill="#f3dede" stroke="#33373d"/>
  <text x="400" y="76" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12">registers</text>
  <text x="400" y="132" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12">shared mem / L1</text>
  <text x="400" y="192" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12">L2 cache</text>
  <text x="400" y="252" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12">HBM — global memory</text>
  <text x="400" y="312" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12">host RAM (over PCIe / NVLink)</text>
  <!-- annotations -->
  <text x="600" y="64" font-size="11" fill="#444b53">per-thread · ~KB · ~1 cycle · fastest</text>
  <text x="600" y="128" font-size="11" fill="#444b53">per-block · ~100 KB · ~tens of cycles</text>
  <text x="600" y="190" font-size="11" fill="#444b53">chip-wide · ~tens of MB</text>
  <text x="600" y="250" font-size="11" fill="#444b53">device · ~tens of GB · ~hundreds of cycles</text>
  <text x="600" y="310" font-size="11" fill="#444b53">system · ~hundreds of GB · slowest</text>
  <text x="160" y="64" font-size="11" fill="#444b53" text-anchor="middle">small</text>
  <text x="160" y="92" font-size="11" fill="#444b53" text-anchor="middle">&amp; fast</text>
  <text x="140" y="308" font-size="11" fill="#444b53" text-anchor="middle">large &amp; slow</text>
  <text x="400" y="366" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" font-style="italic" fill="#444b53">capacities and latencies are representative orders of magnitude, not device-exact</text>
</svg>

The memory hierarchy. Storage close to the arithmetic units (registers,
shared memory) is tiny but nearly free to access; storage far away (HBM, host) is
vast but slow. Performance comes from keeping the working set high in the pyramid
and touching the bottom as rarely as possible.
:::

The numbers that matter are not the absolute figures but the *gaps* between levels,
and they are enormous — global memory is roughly two to three orders of magnitude
slower to reach than a register, and the host is slower still. @fig-memory-cost
plots representative latencies and bandwidths per level on a log scale, which is the
only honest way to show a range this wide.

```{python}
#| label: fig-memory-cost
#| fig-cap: "Representative access latency (left, in clock cycles) and bandwidth (right, relative) across the memory hierarchy, on a log scale. The point is the spread, not the exact values: reaching global HBM costs hundreds of times more than a register, so a kernel's speed is usually set by how much data it moves, not how much it computes."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

levels = ["register", "shared/L1", "L2", "HBM\n(global)", "host\n(PCIe)"]
# Representative orders of magnitude (illustrative, not device-exact).
latency_cycles = [1, 30, 200, 500, 50000]
bandwidth_rel = [20000, 12000, 4000, 2000, 25]   # relative units
colors = ["#3b6fb0", "#3b9f6f", "#d0a02c", "#b03b4b", "#7a5aa0"]

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(10, 3.8))
axL.bar(levels, latency_cycles, color=colors)
axL.set_yscale("log")
axL.set_ylabel("access latency (clock cycles, log)")
axL.set_title("latency: cost of one access")
for i, v in enumerate(latency_cycles):
    axL.text(i, v * 1.3, f"~{v:g}", ha="center", fontsize=8)

axR.bar(levels, bandwidth_rel, color=colors)
axR.set_yscale("log")
axR.set_ylabel("bandwidth (relative, log)")
axR.set_title("bandwidth: how fast bulk data flows")
fig.suptitle("Climbing the memory hierarchy: each step down is hundreds of times slower",
             fontsize=12)
fig.tight_layout(rect=(0, 0, 1, 0.95))
plt.show()
```

This is the origin of the single most important optimisation in GPU numerics:
**move data once, reuse it many times.** If an operation reads a value from HBM,
does one cheap arithmetic step, and writes it back, the arithmetic units sit idle
waiting on memory — the operation is **memory-bound**, and almost all of its time is
HBM traffic. Chaining several such operations, each doing its own HBM round-trip, is
the common pattern the next chapter's **operator fusion** exists to fix: fuse the
chain into one kernel that reads once, does all the arithmetic while the data is
hot in registers, and writes once. The roofline makes this precise.

## The roofline: compute-bound or memory-bound? {#sec-roofline}

For any computation, define its **arithmetic intensity** — the number of
floating-point operations it performs per byte of data it moves to and from main
memory:
$$
I \;=\; \frac{\text{FLOPs}}{\text{bytes moved}}\qquad(\text{FLOP/byte}).
$$
A hardware device has two hard ceilings: a peak compute rate $\pi$ (FLOP/s, set by
the arithmetic units) and a peak memory bandwidth $\beta$ (bytes/s, set by HBM).
The fastest a computation of intensity $I$ can possibly run is whichever ceiling it
hits first:
$$
\text{attainable FLOP/s} \;=\; \min\bigl(\pi,\; \beta \cdot I\bigr).
$$
Plot that on log–log axes and you get the **roofline** (@fig-roofline): a sloped
line $\beta\cdot I$ on the left (you are **memory-bound** — bandwidth limits you,
and going faster means moving fewer bytes per FLOP), a flat ceiling $\pi$ on the
right (you are **compute-bound** — the arithmetic units are saturated), and a
**ridge point** $I^\star=\pi/\beta$ where they meet. To the left of the ridge,
more arithmetic intensity buys speed; to the right, only a faster chip does.

```{python}
#| label: fig-roofline
#| fig-cap: "The roofline. A computation's attainable performance is min(peak compute, bandwidth × arithmetic intensity). Left of the ridge point it is memory-bound (the sloped roof); right of it, compute-bound (the flat roof). Elementwise maps, reductions, and softmax sit in the memory-bound regime — exactly where fusing operations (so data is read once and reused) buys speed; a large matrix multiply is compute-bound. Axes are representative."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

peak = 20e12    # representative fp32 peak, FLOP/s
bw = 2e12       # representative HBM bandwidth, byte/s
ridge = peak / bw   # = 10 FLOP/byte

ai = np.logspace(-2, 3, 500)
roof = np.minimum(peak, bw * ai)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.2, 4.6))
ax.loglog(ai, roof / 1e12, color="#333", lw=2.2, zorder=3)
ax.axvline(ridge, color="0.6", ls=":", lw=1.2)
ax.text(ridge * 1.1, 0.05, f"ridge point\nI* = {ridge:g} FLOP/byte",
        fontsize=8, color="0.3")
ax.fill_betweenx([1e-2, peak / 1e12], 1e-2, ridge, color="#b03b4b", alpha=0.06)
ax.fill_betweenx([1e-2, peak / 1e12], ridge, 1e3, color="#3b6fb0", alpha=0.06)
ax.text(0.03, 13, "memory-bound", color="#b03b4b", fontsize=10)
ax.text(120, 0.4, "compute-bound", color="#3b6fb0", fontsize=10)

ops = [("elementwise / AXPY", 0.12), ("reduction / norm", 0.25),
       ("softmax (row)", 1.5), ("stencil / conv", 5.0),
       ("gemm (large)", 80.0)]
for name, x in ops:
    y = min(peak, bw * x)
    ax.plot(x, y / 1e12, "o", color="#222", ms=6, zorder=4)
    ax.annotate(name, (x, y / 1e12), textcoords="offset points",
                xytext=(6, -12), fontsize=8)

ax.set_xlabel("arithmetic intensity  I  (FLOP / byte)")
ax.set_ylabel("attainable performance (TFLOP/s)")
ax.set_title("The roofline model")
ax.set_ylim(1e-2, peak / 1e12 * 2)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

The roofline is the lens for nearly every performance question in this book.
Elementwise maps, reductions, normalisations, and softmax have low arithmetic
intensity — a few FLOPs per element read — so they live on the sloped roof and are
**memory-bound**; the way to speed them up is to *move less data*, which is exactly
what fusion does. A large matrix multiply does $O(n^3)$ work on $O(n^2)$ data, so
its intensity grows with size and it climbs onto the flat roof to become
**compute-bound** — the regime where tensor cores (next) earn their keep. When the
registration unit reports that its diffeomorphic warps are "bandwidth-bound" and a
fused Pallas kernel wins, or the geometry unit asks whether a sparse matrix-vector
product is worth a custom kernel, they are reading off this diagram.

## Tensor cores: the matrix-multiply units {#sec-tensorcores}

Because matrix multiplication is the compute-bound workhorse of modern numerics,
NVIDIA GPUs since 2017 ship dedicated **tensor cores**: units that compute a small
**matrix multiply-accumulate** $D = A B + C$ in one operation, far faster than
doing the same multiplies on the general arithmetic units. They achieve this partly
by operating on **reduced-precision** inputs — 16-bit (`float16` or `bfloat16`), or
Ampere's 19-bit `TF32`, with newer generations adding 8-bit (`FP8`) and 4-bit
formats — while **accumulating the sum in `float32`**. That last detail is the one
that matters for scientific code: the products may be low precision, but the
running total is not, so a long dot product does not shed precision the way naive
16-bit accumulation would.

This is exactly the line `nitrix` draws. Its scientific core is `float32`/
`float64`; reduced precision is **not** admitted there, because for an
ill-conditioned solve or a curvature estimate it would be a correctness bug.
Reduced precision is allowed only at a narrow neural-network seam — the `attention`,
state-space, and normalisation forward passes — and even there under a hard
invariant that **accumulation stays at least `float32`**. The tensor cores are why
that seam is fast; the accumulation rule is why it is still correct.

## The generations of NVIDIA architecture {#sec-generations}

GPU capability is versioned by **compute capability**, written `sm_XX`, where the
leading digits track the architecture generation. A kernel compiled for one
capability will not necessarily run — or run well — on another, so a library has to
decide which generations it supports. @fig-generations places the recent
generations on a timeline, and the table below records what each one added.

::: {#fig-generations}
<svg viewBox="0 0 820 230" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img"
     aria-label="A timeline of recent NVIDIA architecture generations from Volta to Blackwell, with Ampere and newer highlighted as the range nitrix targets."
     font-family="system-ui, sans-serif" font-size="13">
  <defs>
    <marker id="gah" viewBox="0 0 10 10" refX="9" refY="5" markerWidth="7" markerHeight="7" orient="auto-start-reverse">
      <path d="M0,0 L10,5 L0,10 z" fill="#55606b"/>
    </marker>
  </defs>
  <!-- nitrix-supported band -->
  <rect x="250" y="70" width="540" height="44" fill="#dce9dd" opacity="0.5"/>
  <text x="520" y="60" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#2c6a45" font-style="italic">nitrix targets Ampere and newer (sm_80+)</text>
  <!-- axis -->
  <line x1="40" y1="92" x2="800" y2="92" stroke="#55606b" stroke-width="1.6" marker-end="url(#gah)"/>
  <g text-anchor="middle">
    <!-- ticks -->
    <line x1="120" y1="86" x2="120" y2="98" stroke="#33373d"/><text x="120" y="128">Volta</text><text x="120" y="146" font-size="11" fill="#444b53">sm_70 · 2017</text>
    <line x1="220" y1="86" x2="220" y2="98" stroke="#33373d"/><text x="220" y="128">Turing</text><text x="220" y="146" font-size="11" fill="#444b53">sm_75 · 2018</text>
    <line x1="360" y1="86" x2="360" y2="98" stroke="#33373d"/><text x="360" y="128">Ampere</text><text x="360" y="146" font-size="11" fill="#444b53">sm_80 · 2020</text>
    <line x1="500" y1="86" x2="500" y2="98" stroke="#33373d"/><text x="500" y="128">Ada</text><text x="500" y="146" font-size="11" fill="#444b53">sm_89 · 2022</text>
    <line x1="620" y1="86" x2="620" y2="98" stroke="#33373d"/><text x="620" y="128">Hopper</text><text x="620" y="146" font-size="11" fill="#444b53">sm_90 · 2022</text>
    <line x1="740" y1="86" x2="740" y2="98" stroke="#33373d"/><text x="740" y="128">Blackwell</text><text x="740" y="146" font-size="11" fill="#444b53">sm_100 · 2024</text>
  </g>
  <text x="120" y="180" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#888">first tensor cores</text>
  <text x="360" y="196" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#888">TF32, bf16</text>
  <text x="620" y="180" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#888">FP8, TMA, clusters</text>
  <text x="740" y="196" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#888">FP4/FP6</text>
</svg>

Recent NVIDIA generations. Each adds capability — tensor cores (Volta),
the `TF32` and `bfloat16` formats and structured sparsity
(Ampere), `FP8` with the tensor-memory accelerator and thread-block
clusters (Hopper), and 4/6-bit formats (Blackwell). `nitrix` targets the
shaded range, Ampere and newer.
:::

| Generation | Example GPU | Capability | Key additions | `nitrix` |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Volta | V100 | `sm_70` | first tensor cores (fp16) | JAX fallback |
| Turing | T4 | `sm_75` | int8 tensor cores | JAX fallback |
| **Ampere** | A100 | `sm_80` | `TF32`, `bfloat16`, structured sparsity | **Pallas-Triton** |
| **Ada** | L4, RTX 40xx | `sm_89` | Ampere features, consumer-class | **Pallas-Triton** |
| **Hopper** | H100 | `sm_90` | `FP8`, tensor-memory accelerator, thread-block clusters | Triton; **Mosaic aspirational** |
| **Blackwell** | B200 | `sm_100` | `FP4`/`FP6`, second-gen transformer engine | Triton |

`nitrix` draws its line at **Ampere** (`sm_80`): the internal gate is literally a
constant, `_AMPERE_MIN_MAJOR = 8`, and its hand-written kernels are
[Pallas](https://docs.jax.dev/en/latest/pallas/index.html)-**Triton** programs that
require it. Pre-Ampere NVIDIA cards, and every non-NVIDIA device (TPU, AMD ROCm,
Apple Metal, Intel), fall back to the portable pure-JAX path — the correctness
floor, exercised in CI, where wall-clock speed is explicitly *not* a goal. The
Hopper-and-newer **Pallas-Mosaic** path (which would exploit `FP8`, the
tensor-memory accelerator, and distributed shared memory) is *aspirational*:
the development hardware is an Ada-generation L4, on which the Mosaic GPU backend
does not run, and the project's stance is not to ship a kernel it cannot test.

## What this all means for `nitrix` {#sec-nitrix}

Pull the chapter together and the design of the library's performance layer reads
straight off the hardware.

- **Most of `nitrix` is pure JAX, and lets XLA fuse.** The bulk of the library is
  memory-bound elementwise-and-reduction work — exactly the left side of the
  roofline — so the win is to *move less data*. The [next chapter](xla-jax.qmd)
  shows how the compiler fuses chains of such operations into single kernels that
  read once and write once, climbing the roofline without a line of CUDA.
- **Custom kernels appear only where fusion is not enough.** A handful of
  operations are so bandwidth-bound that a hand-written, register-resident kernel —
  fusing a stencil with its consumer, reusing data the compiler would have spilled
  to HBM — wins decisively *at scale*. These are the Pallas-Triton kernels
  (`attention`, `selective_scan`, `norm`, the `semiring` matmuls, and the
  registration unit's `demons_force` and `lncc_force`), and each fires only when the
  problem is large enough to clear the overhead, the explicit *when-it-pays-off* gate
  the performance chapters describe.
- **Engine selection is explicit and loud.** The **backend** axis from the
  [index](index.qmd#sec-library) is resolved by `resolve_backend`: `auto` chooses the
  fused CUDA path only on Ampere-or-newer NVIDIA hardware (probed once at import) and
  the portable JAX path everywhere else. When a requested fast path cannot run, the
  fallback to JAX is announced with a deduplicated `NitrixBackendFallback` warning —
  because a *silent* performance regression is a bug.
- **The vendor solver is avoided on principle.** cuSOLVER (NVIDIA's dense linear
  algebra) has proven fragile across deployment environments, so `nitrix` rolls its
  own small-matrix factorisations from the operations that *do* work reliably
  (matrix multiply, triangular solve). This recurs throughout the
  [implicit-differentiation](implicit-differentiation.qmd) chapter and the
  statistics unit, and it is the reason a hundred-thousand-voxel solve runs on the
  accelerator at all.

The thread running through every one of these is the one from the
[index](index.qmd#sec-thread-structure): **structure is leverage.** The structure of
the machine — its parallelism, its memory hierarchy, its roofline — is not an
obstacle to work around but a shape to exploit, and a fast library is one whose
computations are cut to fit it.

## Further reading

- Hennessy, J. L. & Patterson, D. A. (2019). *Computer Architecture: A Quantitative
  Approach* (6th ed.). Morgan Kaufmann. — the standard reference on latency vs
  throughput, the memory hierarchy, and data-parallel architectures.
- Williams, S., Waterman, A. & Patterson, D. (2009). "Roofline: an insightful visual
  performance model for multicore architectures." *Communications of the ACM* 52(4).
  — the original roofline paper.
- NVIDIA, *CUDA C++ Programming Guide* and the per-architecture tuning guides
  (Volta through Blackwell) — the authoritative description of the SIMT model,
  warps, occupancy, the memory hierarchy, and tensor cores.
- The [JAX Pallas documentation](https://docs.jax.dev/en/latest/pallas/index.html)
  — the kernel language `nitrix` uses for its Triton (and, aspirationally, Mosaic)
  GPU kernels.

## See also

- Backend resolution and the fallback warning:
  [`_internal/backend.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/_internal/backend.py)
  (`resolve_backend`, `auto_backend`, `NitrixBackendFallback`, `_AMPERE_MIN_MAJOR`).
- The custom GPU kernels:
  [`_kernels/cuda/`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/tree/main/src/nitrix/_kernels/cuda)
  (`attention`, `selective_scan`, `norm`, `semiring_matmul`, `demons_force`,
  `lncc_force`).
- The roofline in action: the [geometry performance chapter](../geometry/performance.qmd)
  (fused-vs-composed kernels, the size gate) and the
  [registration performance chapter](../registration/performance.qmd) (the
  bandwidth-bound diffeomorphic warps and their Pallas force kernels).
- Next chapter: [JAX and XLA](xla-jax.qmd) — how a pure Python function becomes a
  fused kernel on the hardware this chapter described.
